title: EA Process for Agentic Organisations
subtitle: TOGAF Artefacts with Machine-Readable YAML Twins

hero.subline: Every ADM phase produces architecture artefacts — catalogs, matrices, and diagrams that live in the EA Repository. In an agentic organisation, each artefact gains a YAML twin: the same data in a structured format that agents can read, query, and act on.

overview_text: The Architecture Development Method defines what artefacts get produced at each phase — catalogs of elements, matrices of relationships, and diagrams for stakeholders. These artefacts live in the EA Repository. When agents become first-class participants, every artefact gains a YAML twin: a machine-readable version with typed elements, ArchiMate IDs, graph relationships, and agentic extensions. Select a phase to explore its artefacts and generate the YAML twin.

phases.name: Preliminary

process.question: What triggered this architecture initiative?
process.answer: The board-level AI Transformation Programme. The company is a global energy technology leader — $16B revenue, 50,000+ employees, operating across 140+ countries — providing transformers, switchgear, grid automation, HVDC systems, and digital platforms to utilities, transportation, data centres, and industrial customers. There is an existing EA function with medium maturity (Level 2–3): architecture governance exists in pockets, a central repository covers some domains, and domain architects operate within business units. However, architecture practices are highly heterogeneous across five business units and four regions. The CTO has asked: How does the company need to adjust its enterprise architecture to systematically leverage AI — from intelligent grid management and predictive asset maintenance to AI-assisted engineering and agent-enabled operations? This ADM cycle will answer that question and establish the AI-ready architecture capability.
process.input_prompt: What is the business driver or programme that triggers this EA initiative? Who is the executive sponsor?

process.question: Which parts of the organisation are in scope?
process.answer:
All five global business units and four regional organisations plus corporate functions:
Business UnitCore ScopeAI Relevance
Grid AutomationSubstation automation, protection & control, SCADA/EMS platforms, grid management softwareHighest — already operates SCADA, Network Manager, and MicroSCADA platforms with embedded analytics
Grid IntegrationHVDC transmission, power quality, FACTS, offshore wind connectionHigh — HVDC digital control (MACH platform), IdentiQ digital twins, complex system optimisation
High Voltage ProductsSwitchgear, circuit breakers, surge arresters, instrument transformersMedium — quality prediction, design optimisation, predictive maintenance for installed base
TransformersPower transformers, traction transformers, TXpert digital sensorsHigh — TXpert sensor analytics, predictive failure models, digital twin integration
ServiceAsset lifecycle management, consulting, managed services, field operationsHighest — Lumada APM, predictive maintenance, AI-assisted field service, remote monitoring

Regions: Europe (HQ Zurich + major manufacturing/R&D), North America (expansion programme), Asia Pacific (India subsidiary, China hub), Middle East & South America. Each region has different maturity levels and local IT landscapes.

Corporate functions in scope: Group IT, Digital/Lumada Platform, R&D, Cybersecurity, Legal/Compliance.
process.input_prompt: Which business units, divisions, or functions should be included? Are any explicitly out of scope?

process.question: What must the architecture capability achieve?
process.answer:
• Harmonise the architecture landscape across BUs and regions — Today each business unit has its own technology stack, data models, and local architecture practices. Create a unified architecture framework that respects domain-specific needs (IEC 61850 for Grid Automation, HVDC-specific patterns for Grid Integration) while establishing common standards for AI adoption, data sharing, and integration.
  • Define the AI architecture blueprint — Determine where AI creates value across the portfolio: predictive asset health (Lumada APM + TXpert), intelligent grid operations (Network Manager + Nostradamus AI forecasting), AI-assisted engineering (design optimisation for transformers and switchgear), and agent-enabled architecture governance. Map current vs. target state per BU.
  • Establish agent-ready architecture governance — Extend the existing governance structures with AI-specific oversight: agent registration and certification, guardrail definition, audit trails, and human-in-the-loop escalation. Build on the medium maturity baseline — formalise what exists, fill gaps, and add the AI dimension.
  • Move from Level 3 to Level 4 within 18 months — The target is a managed, measurable architecture capability where AI readiness is a first-class dimension of every architecture decision.
process.input_prompt: What are the key objectives? What maturity level are you targeting, and in what timeframe?

process.question: What constraints must we work within?
process.answer:
• Budget: CHF 3.2M over 18 months (Q3 2026 – Q4 2027), drawn from the AI Transformation Programme budget
  • Organisational complexity: Five BUs with distinct product portfolios and P&L accountability, four regions with different regulatory environments, multiple acquired entities with legacy architectures not yet harmonised
  • Regulatory: EU AI Act compliance for all AI/agent deployments, NIS2 directive for critical infrastructure, IEC 62443 cybersecurity certification requirements, NERC CIP for North American grid operations, regional data sovereignty requirements (EU, China, India)
  • Technical landscape: Heterogeneous platform estate — MicroSCADA, Network Manager, Lumada APM, MACH/IdentiQ, TXpert, SAP — running across on-premise, cloud, and edge deployments. Must not disrupt 24/7 grid operations or commissioned HVDC systems
  • Existing EA assets: Partial EA repository (ArchiMate-based, covering ~40% of the application landscape), established ARB in Europe, domain architects in 3 of 5 BUs. These must be built upon, not replaced
  • Talent: Domain architects exist but have limited AI/ML fluency. A dedicated AI/Agent Architect role does not yet exist. Significant competition for AI talent in the energy sector
process.input_prompt: What is the budget and timeline? What regulatory, technical, or organisational constraints apply?

process.question: How will we approach this phase?
process.answer: Six steps, each producing one or more of the deliverables below. The approach builds on existing EA assets rather than starting from scratch:

steps.name: Scope the Enterprise Organisations Impacted
steps.description: Map all 5 BUs and 4 regions against the AI transformation scope. Assess architecture maturity per unit (using the existing partial data), identify heterogeneity hotspots, and confirm Domain Architect assignments — filling gaps in Transformers and High Voltage Products where no architect is currently assigned.

steps.name: Confirm Governance and Support Frameworks
steps.description: Inventory existing governance structures (European ARB, BU-level design authorities, cybersecurity board). Identify regulatory frameworks that constrain AI adoption (EU AI Act, NIS2, IEC 62443, NERC CIP). Extend governance with an AI Ethics Board and agent-specific processes: registration, guardrails, audit, and escalation.

steps.name: Define and Establish the EA Team
steps.description: Formalise the architecture organisation: Chief Enterprise Architect (exists), 5 Domain Architects (3 exist, recruit for Transformers and HV Products), create the new AI/Agent Architect role (responsible for AI architecture standards, agent governance, and Lumada/AI platform alignment), and 2 EA Analysts for repository management.

steps.name: Identify and Establish Architecture Principles
steps.description: Review and extend existing architecture principles for the AI dimension. Define principles spanning: AI Safety (human-in-the-loop for grid-critical decisions), Data Sovereignty (regional compliance), Platform Convergence (Lumada as preferred AI platform), OT/IT Separation (maintaining IEC 62443 zones), and Interoperability (IEC 61850, CIM standards). Each principle gains a machine-readable enforcement rule.

steps.name: Tailor the Architecture Framework
steps.description: Adapt the ADM to the company's federated structure: BU-specific iterations for domain architectures (B, C, D) running in parallel, centralised governance phases (E, F). Define agent participation levels per phase. Introduce AI-specific deliverables: AI Use Case Register, Agent Governance Charter, and Data Readiness Assessment.

steps.name: Extend Tools and Initiate Architecture Work
steps.description: Extend the existing partial EA repository to full coverage — migrate to a graph-native model (Neo4j) with ArchiMate 3.2 metamodel plus AI stereotypes. Integrate with the Lumada platform for asset data. Configure agent access via MCP tools alongside the existing web UI and API channels. Issue the Request for Architecture Work to formally launch the AI Transformation ADM cycle.

process.input_prompt: Are you comfortable with this approach? Should any steps be reordered, combined, or run in parallel?

process.question: How will we measure success?
process.answer:
• EA Maturity: All 5 BUs at Level 3 or above within 12 months; overall capability at Level 4 (Managed & Measurable) within 18 months — up from the current mixed Level 2–3 baseline
  • Architecture Harmonisation: Common reference architecture adopted across all BUs; shared data model covering cross-BU integration points (Lumada, SAP, SCADA/EMS interfaces)
  • AI Blueprint Coverage: AI use cases identified and architecturally assessed for each BU — at least 15 prioritised use cases with target architecture defined
  • Agent Readiness: At least 3 architecture agent roles operational (Architecture Advisory Agent, Compliance Agent, Impact Analysis Agent) by month 12
  • Repository Completeness: Application landscape coverage increased from ~40% to 85%; all cross-BU interfaces documented and queryable
  • Governance Adoption: All BUs participating in quarterly architecture reviews; AI Ethics Board operational with documented agent governance decisions
process.input_prompt: What does success look like? Are these the right metrics, and are the targets realistic?

worked_deliverables.name: Organisational Model for Enterprise Architecture
worked_deliverables.summary: Defines the scope, maturity, roles, constraints, and governance strategy for the EA function across the organisation's five business units and four operating regions, including agent roles as first-class participants in architecture governance.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.content: This deliverable defines who does architecture, where, and how the function is structured across the enterprise. It establishes the scope of organizations impacted by the EA capability, assesses current maturity levels per business unit, defines the team structure and reporting lines (including agent roles), documents constraints and budget, and sets the governance strategy for all architecture activities.

sections.heading: Scope
sections.content:
The EA function spans all five business units, Corporate IT, and the Lumada Digital Platform team. Each unit is assessed for current architecture maturity based on existing capabilities — three BUs already have domain architects in place, and the European ARB has been operational for two years. The scope table reflects this mixed-maturity starting point, not a greenfield setup.

sections.heading: Maturity Assessment
sections.content:
Current-state maturity is assessed across five dimensions using a 1–5 scale (Initial → Optimising). The baseline reflects the organisation's existing capabilities: an operational ARB in Europe, partial repository coverage (~40% of the application landscape catalogued), and domain architects in three of five BUs. Target state reflects the 18-month programme objective of reaching Level 4 overall.

People2.543 of 5 BU domain architects in place; no AI/Agent Architect role; EA analysts needed for graph-based analysis; North America and Asia Pacific lack dedicated EA staff
Content24~40% of application landscape catalogued; architecture artifacts split between SharePoint documents, Sparx EA models, and spreadsheets; no unified graph-native repository
Governance2.54European ARB operational but not replicated in other regions; no agent oversight mechanism; compliance checking is manual and quarterly
Tools24Sparx EA used in Europe (partial); no graph repository; no MCP integration; BUs use different tooling (some have no EA tooling at all)

sections.heading: Constraints
sections.content:
• Budget: CHF 3.2M total programme budget across 18 months (personnel, tooling, consulting, and regional travel)
  • Timeline: 18-month transformation programme with quarterly milestone reviews aligned to existing BU planning cycles
  • Federated Structure: Five BUs operate with P&L accountability and significant autonomy — the EA function must govern through influence and shared standards, not top-down mandate. Each BU retains authority over product-specific architecture decisions
  • Legacy Platform Integration: Must integrate with MicroSCADA X, Network Manager EMS, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, Lumada APM, SAP S/4HANA, and various regional systems — no greenfield replacement of operational platforms
  • Regional Regulatory Divergence: Europe (NIS2 Directive, EU AI Act, GDPR), North America (NERC CIP, state-level regulations), Asia Pacific (India CERT-In, China cybersecurity law), Middle East (varying national standards) — architecture must accommodate different compliance regimes
  • Data Sovereignty: Architecture repository hosted in Zurich; regional processing must respect EU, Chinese, and Indian data residency requirements; agent processing of OT data subject to IEC 62443 zone constraints
  • Agent Boundaries: No autonomous agent actions on grid-critical systems — human-in-the-loop mandatory for protection relay settings, HVDC control parameters, and substation switching operations across all regions

sections.content:

sections.heading: Governance Strategy
sections.content: Architecture governance builds on the existing European ARB and extends it into a globalised, federated governance model. The existing Architecture Review Board (ARB) — currently meeting monthly in Zurich with European scope — is expanded to include all five BU domain architects and regional representatives, with virtual participation from North America and Asia Pacific. The ARB remains chaired by the Chief Enterprise Architect and includes the CTO (sponsor), CISO, and VP of each BU. A new AI Ethics Board is established to oversee agent trust level promotions, review AI use cases for grid-critical applications, and ensure EU AI Act conformance across the organisation's AI portfolio (Nostradamus, Lumada AI modules, architecture agents). BU-level Architecture Councils — already informal in Grid Automation and Service — are formalised across all five BUs with bi-weekly cadence. Quarterly Executive Reviews present maturity progress, risk posture, and agent adoption metrics to the Group Executive Team. Agent oversight follows a graduated trust model: new agents operate in advisory-only mode, graduating to semi-autonomous and then supervised-autonomous as they demonstrate reliability through audit trail analysis. All agent actions are logged to an immutable audit ledger in the graph repository, enabling full traceability from business decision to agent recommendation.

worked_deliverables.name: Tailored Architecture Framework
worked_deliverables.summary: Configures the ADM phases for the organisation's federated structure — centralised governance phases with BU-parallel domain phases — defining agent participation levels, iteration approach respecting BU autonomy, and three new AI-specific deliverables.

sections.heading: ADM Tailoring
sections.content:
graph LR
    classDef central fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#6366f1,color:#312e81
    classDef bupar fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#166534

    P --> A --> B --> C --> D --> EF --> G --> H

Central (Chief EA) BU-Parallel (Domain Architects) Cross-BU Merged

The framework is tailored for a federated operating model: governance phases (Preliminary, A, G, H) run centrally under the Chief Enterprise Architect, while domain phases (B, C, D) run in parallel across the five BUs with their respective domain architects leading. This respects P&L autonomy while ensuring architectural coherence. Phases E/F are merged and run as cross-BU workshops to identify shared solution patterns and coordinate migration sequencing.

A — Architecture VisionCentral — cross-BU workshopsWaterfall with workshops in Zurich + virtual for NA/APACMedium — agent drafts cross-BU stakeholder analysis, identifies concern patterns across regions
B — Business ArchitectureBU-parallel — each BU domain architect leadsAgile sprints (2-week) per BU, synchronised at sprint boundariesHigh — agent generates BU capability maps, performs cross-BU gap analysis, identifies shared capabilities
C — Information Systems (Data)BU-parallel — coordinated by Corporate ITAgile sprints (2-week) per BUHigh — agent discovers data flows across Lumada, SAP, MicroSCADA; generates entity catalogs from schema analysis
C — Information Systems (Application)BU-parallel — coordinated by Corporate ITAgile sprints (2-week) per BUHigh — agent maps application portfolio across BUs (~40% already catalogued), identifies redundancy across 140+ country installations
D — Technology ArchitectureBU-parallel — OT/IT splitAgile sprints (2-week) per BUHigh — agent models infrastructure topology per region, performs IEC 62443 zone analysis across OT/IT boundaries
E/F — Opportunities & MigrationCentral — cross-BU integration workshopsMerged phases, sprint-based delivery planningMedium — agent generates dependency graphs for migration sequencing, proposes shared platform consolidation
G — Implementation GovernanceCentral — globalised ARB oversightContinuous (aligned to BU delivery cadences)High — agent monitors compliance continuously across all BUs, flags deviations from shared standards
H — Architecture Change ManagementCentral — event-drivenContinuous (triggered by BU or platform changes)High — agent detects change triggers from Lumada/SAP/MicroSCADA, performs cross-BU impact analysis

SAP S/4HANA ConnectorMaster data synchronisation — organisational structure, application catalogCorporate IT, Repository AgentOData API, scheduled sync with conflict detection

sections.heading: Mandatory Deliverables
sections.content:
Each phase produces a defined set of deliverables. All deliverables are stored in the graph repository with a YAML twin for agent consumption. BU-parallel phases produce one deliverable set per BU plus a cross-BU integration view.

  • Preliminary: Org Model, Tailored Framework, Initial Repository, Principles Catalog, Business Context, Governance Framework, Request for Architecture Work, EA Capability Architecture
  • Phase A: Architecture Vision, Stakeholder Map (including BU and regional stakeholders), Statement of Architecture Work, Communications Plan (multi-region)
  • Phase B (per BU): Business Architecture Definition, BU Capability Map, Process Catalog, Gap Analysis + Cross-BU Shared Capability Map
  • Phase C (Data, per BU): Data Architecture Definition, Data Entity Catalog, Data Flow Diagrams + Cross-BU Data Integration Map (Lumada/SAP/MicroSCADA flows)
  • Phase C (App, per BU): Application Architecture Definition, Application Portfolio, Integration Matrix + Group Application Rationalisation View
  • Phase D (per BU): Technology Architecture Definition, Infrastructure Topology, OT/IT Boundary Model per region + Group Network Architecture
  • Phase E/F: Solutions Architecture, Migration Plan, Transition Architectures (coordinated across BU timelines)
  • Phase G: Compliance Assessments (per region and regulatory regime), Architecture Contracts, Implementation Reviews
  • Phase H: Change Requests, Impact Assessments, Updated Architecture Definitions

sections.heading: New Agentic Deliverables
sections.content:
Three deliverables are introduced specifically for the agentic operating model. These have no traditional EA equivalent and are essential for governing AI agent participation across the organisation.

DeliverablePhase ProducedDescription
AI Use Case RegisterPhase A (updated each cycle)Catalogues all AI use cases across the five BUs — from Nostradamus forecasting in Grid Integration to Lumada APM predictive maintenance in Service — with EU AI Act risk classification, data requirements, and architecture dependencies. Each use case links to affected ArchiMate elements in the repository
Agent Governance CharterPreliminaryDefines agent registration process, trust levels (advisory → semi-autonomous → supervised-autonomous), escalation rules, and audit requirements. Includes specific provisions for grid-critical agent boundaries aligned to IEC 62443 security zones and NERC CIP requirements
Data Readiness AssessmentPhase B/C (per BU)Evaluates each BU's data readiness for AI consumption: data quality scores, schema completeness, real-time availability, labelling coverage, and integration pathway to the graph repository. Covers critical data sources — MicroSCADA telemetry, TXpert sensor streams, Lumada APM asset records, SAP master data

sections.heading: Decision Log Format
sections.content: All architecture decisions — whether made by humans or proposed by agents — follow a structured format stored in the graph repository. In a federated structure, decisions are scoped: BU-local decisions are approved by the BU Architecture Council, while cross-BU decisions (affecting shared platforms like Lumada or SAP, or establishing group-wide standards) require ARB approval. Each decision record captures: Decision ID (auto-generated, sequential), Title (concise statement), Context (what prompted the decision), Scope (BU-local or cross-BU, with affected regions), Options Considered (at least two alternatives with trade-offs), Decision (the chosen option with rationale), Consequences (positive and negative impacts), Status (proposed → approved → superseded), Decision Maker (human role or agent ID with human approver), and Linked Elements (ArchiMate element IDs affected by the decision). Agent-proposed decisions carry an additional confidence score and a link to the evidence trail in the graph that supports the recommendation.

worked_deliverables.name: Initial Architecture Repository
worked_deliverables.summary: Extends the organisation's existing partial architecture repository (~40% application landscape coverage in Sparx EA) by migrating to a Neo4j graph database, loading BU-specific reference models and platform catalogs, and enabling three access channels including 32 MCP tools for agent consumption.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.content: This deliverable establishes the architecture repository as the single source of truth for all architecture knowledge across the enterprise. It defines the graph database architecture, initial content loaded from existing sources and reference models, and the access channels that serve human architects, AI agents, and CI/CD pipelines.

sections.heading: Repository Architecture
sections.content:
graph TD
    classDef channel fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#6366f1,color:#312e81
    classDef core fill:#1e293b,stroke:#475569,color:#f1f5f9
    classDef source fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#16a34a,color:#166534
    WEB["Web Portal (Architects)"]:::channel
    MCP["MCP Server (32 Agent Tools)"]:::channel
    API["REST API (CI/CD Pipelines)"]:::channel
    NEO["Neo4j Graph DB — ArchiMate 3.2"]:::core
    WEB --> NEO
    MCP --> NEO
    API --> NEO
    SPARX["Sparx EA Migration (~380 elements)"]:::source --> NEO
    PLAT["Platform Catalog (~120 elements)"]:::source --> NEO
    REF["Reference Models (IEC 61850, CIM, 62443)"]:::source --> NEO
    REG["Regulatory Catalog (NIS2, NERC CIP)"]:::source --> NEO

The architecture repository builds on the existing partial catalog rather than starting from scratch. The current state — approximately 40% of the application landscape documented in Sparx EA (primarily European installations), with additional architecture artifacts scattered across SharePoint, Confluence, and BU-specific spreadsheets — is migrated to a Neo4j graph database with the full ArchiMate 3.2 metamodel. The graph schema comprises 60 element types across 7 architectural layers, approximately 300 relationship rules enforced as database constraints, and 8 AI-specific stereotypes for agent-related elements (AgentRole, AgentService, MLModel, DataPipeline, GuardrailPolicy, TrustBoundary, MCPEndpoint, DigitalTwin).

Migration is phased: existing Sparx EA models are exported as ArchiMate Exchange Format and loaded into Neo4j first (preserving element IDs and relationships). SharePoint documents and spreadsheets are then parsed by a migration agent to extract and structure architecture elements. The graph structure enables native traversal queries — cross-BU impact analysis that would require manually correlating multiple Sparx models becomes a single Cypher path query. Every element carries lifecycle metadata (status, version, last-modified-by, originating BU, region) and provenance tracking (migrated-from-sparx, created-by-human, or proposed-by-agent with confidence score).

sections.heading: Initial Content
sections.content:
The repository is pre-loaded with both migrated content from existing tools and new reference models specific to the organisation's product lines and operating standards.

Sparx EA MigrationExisting repository (Europe)~40% of the application landscape, plus partial business capability models from Grid Automation and Service BUs~380 elements

IEC 61850 Reference ModelInternational Electrotechnical CommissionSubstation automation data model — objects for switchgear, transformers, protection relays (Relion), used by Grid Automation and HV Products142 elements
CIM (IEC 61970/61968)International Electrotechnical CommissionCommon Information Model for grid topology — network connectivity, equipment types, measurement points; foundation for Network Manager EMS203 elements

Regulatory Compliance CatalogLegal & ComplianceNERC CIP requirements (North America), NIS2 Directive obligations (Europe), EU AI Act classification rules, regional standards per operating country87 elements

Additionally loaded: BU Reference Models (one capability reference model per BU, derived from existing documentation), Standards Catalog (IEEE C57 for transformers, IEC 62351 security, ISO 27001), and Template Library (viewpoint templates for 15 ArchiMate viewpoints, decision record templates, agent registration forms).

sections.heading: Access Model
sections.content:
The repository is accessible through three distinct channels, reflecting the needs of the organisation's diverse user base across regions and BUs.

ChannelConsumerMechanismCapabilities
Web UIArchitects, BU stakeholders, regional IT leadsWeb dashboard with graph visualisation, search, and viewpoint rendering — replaces Sparx EA as the primary human interfaceBrowse elements by BU/region/layer, explore cross-BU relationships, generate viewpoint diagrams, run maturity assessments per BU, export compliance reports per regulatory regime
MCP ToolsRegistered AI agents (architecture agents, compliance agent, Nostradamus integration)32 MCP tools exposed via Model Context Protocol server8 query tools (element search, path traversal, cross-BU pattern matching), 8 mutation tools (create/update/link elements with BU scoping), 8 analysis tools (impact analysis, cross-BU gap detection, compliance check per regulatory regime), 8 validation tools (schema conformance, IEC 62443 zone validation, completeness scoring per BU)
CI/CD APIDevOps pipelines, Lumada platform, SAP integration layerREST endpoints with OpenAPI specificationBulk import from Lumada APM asset registry, SAP master data sync, webhook triggers on architecture changes for downstream CI/CD pipelines, schema validation for incoming YAML twins, integration with Jira and ServiceNow

sections.heading: Migration Plan
sections.content:
Migration from the existing Sparx EA repository follows a phased approach to minimise disruption and ensure data quality.

PhaseTimelineScopeMethod
Phase 1: Schema SetupMonth 1Full ArchiMate 3.2 metamodel in Neo4j — 60 element types, ~300 relationship rules, 8 AI stereotypes, 15 viewpoint templatesAutomated schema deployment from metamodel definition
Phase 2: Sparx EA MigrationMonth 1–2~380 existing elements exported from Sparx EA (Europe) — primarily application components, business processes, and partial capability modelsArchiMate Exchange Format export; Migration Agent validates, cleanses, and loads; parallel running for 6 months
Phase 3: Platform CatalogMonth 2–3~120 elements from BU platform inventories — Lumada, MicroSCADA X, Network Manager EMS, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, SAPBU Domain Architects validate; API-based sync configured for Lumada and SAP
Phase 4: Reference ModelsMonth 3–4~500 elements from IEC standards and regulatory frameworks — IEC 61850 (142), CIM (203), IEC 62443 (64), Regulatory Catalog (87)Structured import from standard model definitions
Phase 5: BU ExtensionMonth 4–9New content from BU-parallel ADM phases (B, C, D) — capability maps, data models, application portfolios, technology topologiesAgents generate initial drafts; BU Domain Architects review and refine

Parallel Running: Sparx EA remains available in read-only mode for 6 months after Phase 2. All new content is created exclusively in Neo4j. Migration success measured by element coverage (target: 100% migrated) and user adoption (all architects using Neo4j within 3 months).

sections.heading: Repository Governance
sections.content:
Repository governance ensures data quality, consistency, and completeness across all five BUs and four regions.

  • Element Ownership: Every element has a designated owner (BU Domain Architect for BU-specific elements, Chief EA for cross-BU). Owners are responsible for accuracy, currency, and lifecycle management
  • Change Control: All mutations tracked with full provenance — timestamp, actor (human or agent ID), change type, affected elements, justification. Agent-proposed changes carry a confidence score and evidence trail
  • Quality Standards: Repository Agent monitors quality continuously — flagging orphan elements, stale metadata (not updated in 6 months), missing mandatory properties, and naming convention violations. Quality metrics reported per BU at monthly ARB meetings
  • Access Control: Role-based access aligned to the federated governance model. BU Domain Architects: full access to BU elements, read all. AI/Agent Architect: manages agent-related elements. EA Analysts: repository-wide maintenance. Agents: MCP access with scope constraints defined at registration
  • Versioning: Point-in-time snapshots at phase boundaries (baseline, target, transition architectures). Each snapshot is immutable and queryable independently for change comparison and audit

worked_deliverables.name: Principles Catalog
worked_deliverables.summary: Ten architecture principles governing all design decisions across the organisation's five BUs and four regions, each with machine-readable enforcement rules that agents evaluate automatically against the graph repository.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.content: The Principles Catalog establishes the architecture principles that govern all design decisions across the enterprise. Each principle carries a statement, rationale, implications, and a machine-readable enforcement rule that agents evaluate programmatically against every architecture decision and change request. The ten principles span four domains: Safety & Security, Platform & Technology, Data & Integration, and Governance & Compliance.

sections.heading: Architecture Principles
sections.content:
Each principle carries a statement, rationale, implications, and — critically — an enforcement mechanism that agents can evaluate programmatically against every architecture decision and change request. These principles apply across all five BUs and all operating regions, with regional adaptations noted where regulatory environments differ.

PrincipleStatementRationaleImplicationsEnforcement
AI Safety (Human-in-the-Loop)All AI and agent actions affecting grid-critical systems — protection relays, HVDC control, substation switching — require human approval before executionGrid infrastructure serves millions of customers across 140+ countries; autonomous AI errors on Relion relays or MicroSCADA could cascade into safety incidents. NERC CIP and IEC 62443 mandate human oversight for critical operationsAgents cannot autonomously modify protection settings, HVDC parameters, or load-shedding configurations; MicroSCADA X and Network Manager EMS integrations require human confirmation gatesAgent evaluates action scope against IEC 62443 zone model — blocks execution and escalates to domain architect if action touches any element within OT security zones
Platform ConvergenceLumada is the preferred platform for asset management, field service, and industrial IoT across all BUs — new solutions must justify why Lumada cannot serve the need before selecting alternativesFive BUs have historically selected platforms independently, creating redundant capabilities and integration overhead; Lumada APM/EAM/FSM provides a unified foundation that reduces total cost of ownershipNew asset management, field service, or IoT initiatives evaluate Lumada first; non-Lumada choices require ARB exception with documented justification and integration planAgent checks new ApplicationComponent elements in asset/field-service/IoT domains — flags non-Lumada selections without approved exception in the repository
OT/IT SeparationOperational Technology and Information Technology domains maintain defined security boundaries per IEC 62443 zones, with controlled integration points through DMZ infrastructureMicroSCADA X, Relion relays, and HVDC control systems manage physical grid infrastructure; compromised IT-to-OT pathways risk safety incidents across all BUs and regulatory violations in every operating regionAll OT/IT data exchange flows through a DMZ with protocol translation; no direct IT-to-OT network connectivity; each BU maintains its own zone model aligned to the group-wide IEC 62443 frameworkAgent validates network topology — flags any direct ACCESS relationship crossing the OT/IT boundary without DMZ intermediary; validates zone assignments per BU
Data SovereigntyAll data processing and storage complies with the data residency requirements of each operating region — EU (GDPR, NIS2), North America (state-level), Asia Pacific (India CERT-In, China cybersecurity law), Middle East (national standards)Operating in 140+ countries with divergent data protection regimes; the organisation must demonstrate data location control per jurisdiction, not just EU complianceData location must be verified for every new service per region; cross-border data flows require Data Protection Impact Assessment; architecture repository hosted in Zurich; Chinese and Indian operations may require local processing nodesAgent checks Node hosting location property against approved regions list per jurisdiction — rejects deployments that violate regional data residency rules
Interoperability StandardsAll system interfaces conform to established energy sector standards — IEC 61850 for substation automation, IEC 61970/61968 (CIM) for grid topology, IEEE C57 for transformer data exchangeThe organisation's products (MicroSCADA X, Network Manager EMS, Relion, TXpert) must interoperate with third-party equipment across customer installations worldwide; proprietary interfaces create lock-inNew MicroSCADA integrations must use IEC 61850; grid management uses CIM; transformer monitoring uses IEEE C57-aligned data models; all BU product interfaces are published to the standards catalogAgent checks interface specifications against standards catalog — flags non-standard protocols and requires justification with customer-facing impact assessment
API FirstAll capabilities are exposed through well-defined, versioned APIs before any user interface is built — enabling agent access, cross-BU integration, and customer ecosystem participationFive BUs and four regions need to integrate without point-to-point coupling; agents require programmatic access to all systems; customers increasingly demand API access to asset data (Lumada, TXpert)Every service publishes an OpenAPI specification in the platform catalog; no direct database integrations between BU systems; Lumada, MicroSCADA, and SAP expose APIs for agent consumptionAgent validates that every ApplicationService has at least one associated API specification artifact in the repository
Secure by DesignSecurity controls aligned to IEC 62443 are embedded in architecture from inception — threat modelling, secure coding, and penetration testing are mandatory, not optional add-onsThe organisation's products are deployed in critical national infrastructure across 140+ countries; NIS2 (Europe), NERC CIP (North America), and national regulations demand demonstrable security by designThreat modelling is mandatory for every new solution building block; IEC 62443 security level assignments required for all OT-facing components; security review gates in all ADM phases; regional CISO sign-off per operating regionAgent checks that every SolutionBuildingBlock has associated security controls, an IEC 62443 security level assignment, and a completed threat model artifact
Cloud-SelectiveIT workloads default to cloud deployment for scalability and cost efficiency, while OT workloads and grid-critical systems remain on-premises or at the edge — no blanket cloud mandateLumada analytics, SAP, and corporate IT benefit from cloud elasticity; MicroSCADA, Relion, and HVDC control systems require deterministic latency and air-gapped security that cloud cannot guaranteeNew IT projects evaluate cloud-native first; OT systems remain on-premises by default; edge computing for substations and HVDC stations; hybrid architecture patterns for Lumada (cloud analytics + edge data collection)Agent checks deployment target — IT workloads without cloud evaluation flagged; OT workloads with cloud deployment flagged for safety review
Digital Twin FirstEvery significant physical asset — transformers, HVDC converters, substations, switchgear — has a digital twin representation before or upon operational deploymentDigital twins (IdentiQ for HVDC, TXpert for transformers) enable predictive maintenance, scenario simulation, and agent-assisted optimisation; the organisation already has twin platforms that need architectural integrationTransformer deployments require TXpert digital twin linkage; HVDC stations require IdentiQ twin; substations require digital twin in Lumada APM; twin must be linked to real-time sensor feedsAgent verifies that PhysicalComponent elements of type grid-asset have an associated DigitalTwin stereotype element with active data feed relationships to TXpert, IdentiQ, or Lumada APM
Continuous ComplianceArchitecture compliance is assessed continuously by agents against all applicable regulatory regimes, not periodically by manual review — with regime-specific rule sets per operating regionOperating in 140+ countries with multiple regulatory regimes (NERC CIP, NIS2, EU AI Act, regional standards); quarterly manual reviews cannot keep pace with the rate of architecture change across five BUsAll architecture changes trigger automated compliance evaluation per applicable regime; non-compliance blocks promotion to production; compliance dashboard provides real-time posture per BU and regionAgent runs regime-specific compliance rule sets on every repository mutation — generates compliance report per applicable regulation and blocks non-conformant changes

yaml.content:
schema: ea-repository/v1
metadata:
  title: Principles Catalog
  organisation: Global Energy Technology Group
  phase: Preliminary
  status: approved
  created: 2025-03-15
  owner: Marcus Lindberg

elements:
  - id: principle-ai-safety
    type: Principle
    name: AI Safety (Human-in-the-Loop)
    description: Grid-critical AI/agent actions require human approval
    properties:
      statement: All AI/agent actions affecting grid-critical systems require human approval
      rationale: NERC CIP, IEC 62443 mandate human oversight for critical operations
      implications: Agents cannot modify protection settings, HVDC params, or switching configs
      enforcement_rule: action.scope INTERSECT iec62443_ot_zones == EMPTY OR has_human_approval == true
      enforcement_type: automated-with-escalation
      scope: AgentAction

  - id: principle-platform-convergence
    type: Principle
    name: Platform Convergence (Lumada Preferred)
    description: Lumada is the preferred platform for asset management, field service, and IoT
    properties:
      statement: New solutions must justify why Lumada cannot serve the need before alternatives
      rationale: Reduce BU-level platform redundancy and integration overhead
      implications: Non-Lumada choices require ARB exception with justification
      enforcement_rule: "ApplicationComponent[domain IN (asset-mgmt, field-service, iot)].platform == 'Lumada' OR has_approved_exception == true"
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: ApplicationComponent

  - id: principle-ot-it-separation
    type: Principle
    name: OT/IT Separation (IEC 62443 Zones)
    description: OT and IT maintain defined IEC 62443 security boundaries
    properties:
      statement: OT/IT domains maintain IEC 62443 zone boundaries with DMZ integration points
      rationale: MicroSCADA, Relion, HVDC control manage physical grid; IT compromise must not reach OT
      implications: All OT/IT exchange through DMZ; each BU maintains zone model
      enforcement_rule: path(IT_zone, OT_zone).passes_through(DMZ) == true
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: NetworkPath

  - id: principle-data-sovereignty
    type: Principle
    name: Data Sovereignty (Multi-Region)
    description: Data processing complies with each operating region's residency requirements
    properties:
      statement: Data residency per jurisdiction — EU (GDPR/NIS2), NA, APAC (India/China), ME
      rationale: 140+ countries with divergent data protection regimes
      implications: Per-region verification; cross-border flows require DPIA; repo in Zurich
      enforcement_rule: node.hosting_region IN approved_regions[node.jurisdiction]
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: Node

  - id: principle-interop-standards
    type: Principle
    name: Interoperability Standards
    description: All interfaces conform to IEC 61850, CIM, IEEE C57
    properties:
      statement: System interfaces conform to IEC 61850, IEC 61970/61968 (CIM), IEEE C57
      rationale: Products must interoperate with third-party equipment worldwide
      implications: MicroSCADA uses IEC 61850; grid management uses CIM; transformers use IEEE C57
      enforcement_rule: interface.protocol IN standards_catalog.approved_protocols
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: Interface

  - id: principle-api-first
    type: Principle
    name: API First
    description: All capabilities exposed through versioned APIs for integration and agent access
    properties:
      statement: Capabilities exposed through versioned APIs before UI — enabling agents and cross-BU integration
      rationale: Five BUs, four regions, agents all need programmatic access
      implications: Every service publishes OpenAPI spec; no direct DB integrations between BUs
      enforcement_rule: count(related(ApplicationService, API_SPEC)) >= 1
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: ApplicationService

  - id: principle-secure-by-design
    type: Principle
    name: Secure by Design (IEC 62443)
    description: Security controls embedded from inception per IEC 62443
    properties:
      statement: IEC 62443-aligned security controls embedded from inception, not retrofitted
      rationale: Products in critical national infrastructure across 140+ countries; NIS2/NERC CIP demand it
      implications: Threat modelling mandatory; IEC 62443 security level assignments for OT components
      enforcement_rule: count(related(SBB, SecurityControl)) >= 1 AND has_threat_model AND has_iec62443_sl
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: SolutionBuildingBlock

  - id: principle-cloud-selective
    type: Principle
    name: Cloud-Selective
    description: IT defaults to cloud; OT stays on-premises or at the edge
    properties:
      statement: IT workloads cloud-first; OT/grid-critical systems on-premises or edge — no blanket mandate
      rationale: Lumada analytics benefits from cloud; MicroSCADA/Relion/HVDC need deterministic latency
      implications: IT evaluates cloud-native; OT on-premises default; edge for substations/HVDC
      enforcement_rule: "component.domain == 'IT' -> evaluate_cloud(); component.domain == 'OT' -> flag_if_cloud()"
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: ApplicationComponent

  - id: principle-digital-twin
    type: Principle
    name: Digital Twin First
    description: Every significant physical asset has a digital twin
    properties:
      statement: Transformers, HVDC converters, substations, switchgear have digital twins upon deployment
      rationale: IdentiQ (HVDC), TXpert (transformers), Lumada APM (substations) already provide twin capability
      implications: Transformer -> TXpert twin; HVDC -> IdentiQ twin; substation -> Lumada APM twin
      enforcement_rule: "PhysicalComponent[type=grid-asset].has_related(DigitalTwin) AND twin.has_data_feed == true"
      enforcement_type: automated
      scope: PhysicalComponent

  - id: principle-continuous-compliance
    type: Principle
    name: Continuous Compliance
    description: Compliance assessed continuously with regime-specific rule sets per region
    properties:
      statement: Agent-assessed compliance per regulatory regime, not periodic manual review
      rationale: 140+ countries, multiple regimes; quarterly reviews cannot keep pace across 5 BUs
      implications: Per-mutation compliance evaluation; regime-specific rules; real-time dashboard
      enforcement_rule: on_mutation(ANY) -> run_regime_rules(element.region) -> block_if_non_conformant()
      enforcement_type: automated-event-driven
      scope: Repository

worked_deliverables.name: Business Principles, Goals, and Drivers
worked_deliverables.summary: Captures the organisation's business principles, measurable strategic goals tied to the AI Transformation Programme, and the external drivers compelling architecture transformation across all five BUs and four operating regions.

sections.heading: Purpose
sections.content: This deliverable captures the business context that drives all architecture work — the foundational principles that express the organisation's values, the measurable strategic goals tied to the AI Transformation Programme, and the external drivers compelling architecture transformation. These elements are the upstream inputs that shape every architecture decision across all five BUs and four operating regions.

sections.heading: Business Context
sections.content: The architecture initiative operates within the context of a global energy technology leader — $16B revenue, 50,000+ employees, 140+ countries — at a strategic inflection point. Three forces converge: the global energy transition demands new grid infrastructure (HVDC, renewables integration, distributed energy), AI disruption is transforming how assets are monitored and operated, and regulatory pressure (EU AI Act, NIS2, NERC CIP) requires systematic governance of AI deployments. The CTO has asked: How does the company need to adjust its enterprise architecture to systematically leverage AI? The business principles, goals, and drivers below define the answer space for that question.

sections.heading: Business Principles
sections.content:
Five foundational business principles shape every architecture decision across all BUs and regions. These are distinct from architecture principles — they express the organisation's core values and operating philosophy as a global energy technology provider.

PrincipleStatementImplications for Architecture
Safety FirstNo operational decision — whether made by a human or an agent — may compromise grid safety, worker safety, or public safety. This applies to all products (MicroSCADA X, Relion, HVDC systems) and all customer installationsAll agent actions affecting physical infrastructure require safety assessment; human-in-the-loop mandatory for protection relay configuration, HVDC control, and substation switching across all BUs and regions
Operational ExcellenceProducts and services must deliver world-class reliability; the organisation's reputation rests on the uptime and performance of installed grid infrastructure in 140+ countriesArchitecture must support Lumada APM for real-time monitoring, TXpert for predictive transformer maintenance, and automated failover patterns; no single point of failure in customer-facing systems
Sustainable Energy FutureEvery technology choice and product development decision actively supports the global energy transition — enabling renewable integration, grid flexibility, and electrificationArchitecture must accommodate HVDC Light for offshore wind connection, e-mesh for distributed energy resources, virtual power plant orchestration via Network Manager EMS, and grid storage integration
Customer CentricityGrid operators, utilities, and industrial customers worldwide receive integrated solutions, transparent lifecycle support, and measurable performance outcomes through EnCompass partnershipsArchitecture enables Lumada-based asset health visibility for customers, remote monitoring as a service, predictive maintenance insights, and API-accessible operational data
Technology LeadershipThe organisation leads in power grid technology and digital innovation — pioneering AI-augmented grid management, digital twins, and intelligent automation that define the next generation of energy infrastructureArchitecture provides AI experimentation infrastructure (Nostradamus, Lumada AI), agent framework for rapid prototyping, and IdentiQ/TXpert digital twin platforms as innovation accelerators

sections.heading: Strategic Goals
sections.content:
Each strategic goal is measurable, time-bound, and linked to a KPI that agents can monitor and report on. These goals are aligned to the CTO-sponsored AI Transformation Programme and apply across all five BUs.

GoalTargetTarget DateKPIAgent Monitoring
AI Adoption Across BUsAt least 3 production AI use cases per BU (15 total across Grid Automation, Grid Integration, HV Products, Transformers, Service)2027-Q2Number of production AI use cases per BUAgent tracks AI Use Case Register, flags BUs below target trajectory
Cross-BU Platform Integration80% of BU applications connected to Lumada platform or accessible via standard APIs2027-Q4% of application portfolio with API or Lumada integrationAgent monitors application portfolio API coverage and Lumada integration status per BU
Platform ConvergenceReduce application redundancy across BUs by 30% — consolidating overlapping tools onto shared platforms (Lumada, SAP, common analytics)2027-Q4Number of redundant applications eliminated; total application count reductionAgent performs cross-BU application portfolio overlap analysis quarterly
Architecture Talent DevelopmentDomain architects in all 5 BUs (currently 3/5), AI/Agent Architect role filled, 10+ staff trained in graph-native EA methods2026-Q4EA role fill rate; architecture training completion rateAgent tracks role assignments in org model, flags vacancies and training gaps
Continuous Compliance Posture100% of architecture changes assessed automatically against applicable regulatory regimes (NIS2, NERC CIP, EU AI Act) with zero manual-only assessments2027-Q2% of changes with automated compliance assessment; manual assessment backlogAgent monitors compliance assessment coverage, escalates manual-only assessments

sections.heading: Business Drivers
sections.content:
Six external drivers compel the architecture transformation. The central question — how does the company need to adjust to leverage AI? — runs through every driver. Each is assessed for urgency and directly linked to architecture responses.

DriverDescriptionUrgencyArchitecture Response
Energy TransitionGovernment mandates worldwide — EU Green Deal, US IRA, China dual-carbon goals — drive massive investment in renewables, requiring HVDC transmission (offshore wind), grid flexibility (FACTS), and DER management (e-mesh). The organisation's Grid Integration and Grid Automation BUs are at the centre of this transformationCriticalHVDC Light architecture patterns, e-mesh DER management integration, Network Manager EMS capacity for bi-directional flows, virtual power plant orchestration
Grid Modernisation DemandUtilities worldwide are investing in digital grid infrastructure — sensors, smart substations, automated protection — creating unprecedented demand for MicroSCADA X, Relion, and Network Manager EMS. The architecture must enable rapid deployment across customer installationsCriticalStandardised substation architecture patterns, MicroSCADA X/Relion integration templates, TXpert sensor deployment architecture, digital twin platform (IdentiQ, Lumada APM)
AI DisruptionAI is transforming how grid assets are monitored, maintained, and optimised. Competitors are deploying AI for predictive maintenance, autonomous grid management, and design optimisation. The organisation must embed AI across all five BUs — not just in isolated innovation projectsCriticalNostradamus AI integration architecture, Lumada APM predictive models, agent-assisted architecture governance, AI infrastructure patterns for each BU, EU AI Act compliance framework
Talent ShortageExperienced power systems engineers retiring across all regions; 50,000+ employees but critical knowledge concentrated in aging workforce. Simultaneously, the organisation needs AI/ML engineers and data scientists that are in global short supplyHighKnowledge capture in graph repository, agent-assisted operations for less experienced staff, automated playbook execution via MicroSCADA, AI-augmented engineering design tools
Regulatory PressureEU AI Act (effective 2025–2026), NIS2 Directive (critical infrastructure), NERC CIP (North America), plus national regulations in India, China, Middle East — each BU and region faces different compliance requirements that architecture must accommodate simultaneouslyHighMulti-regime continuous compliance framework, automated audit trail per jurisdiction, agent-verified security posture, IEC 62443 zone architecture, EU AI Act risk classification of all AI use cases
Customer Demand for Digital ServicesUtilities and industrial customers increasingly expect digital services — remote asset monitoring, predictive maintenance insights, API-accessible operational data — alongside physical products. EnCompass partnerships require digital enablement architectureHighLumada-as-a-Service architecture, remote monitoring infrastructure, customer-facing API layer, TXpert/IdentiQ digital twin access for customers, data sharing governance framework

worked_deliverables.name: Architecture Governance Framework
worked_deliverables.summary: Extends the existing European ARB into a globalised, federated governance model spanning all five BUs and four regions, with a new AI Ethics Board, formalised BU-level Architecture Councils, agent governance mechanisms, and a RACI matrix reflecting the organisation's federated structure.

sections.heading: Governance Philosophy
sections.content: Architecture governance follows a federated model that balances enterprise-wide coherence with business unit autonomy. The organising principle: BU autonomy for BU-scoped decisions, central governance for cross-BU and platform decisions. This reflects the organisation's structure — five BUs with P&L accountability that must retain authority over product-specific architecture while adhering to shared standards for AI adoption, platform convergence, and regulatory compliance. Governance is exercised through influence and shared standards, not top-down mandate. Agent-enabled governance extends this model by providing continuous compliance monitoring and cross-BU impact analysis that would be impractical through manual processes alone.

sections.heading: Governance Bodies
sections.content:

sections.heading: Governance Processes
sections.content:
Four core processes ensure architecture conformance and manage change across the federated structure. The key design principle: BU autonomy for BU-scoped decisions, central governance for cross-BU and platform decisions.

  • Architecture Compliance Review: Triggered when any solution building block enters design phase in any BU. The Compliance Agent runs automated rule checks against the principles catalog and the applicable regulatory regime (NIS2 for European deployments, NERC CIP for North American, etc.). Human architects review the agent's compliance report and approve or request remediation. Results are stored as typed relationships in the graph repository, tagged by BU and region.
  • Exception Process: When a design cannot conform to a principle (e.g., non-Lumada platform choice, on-premises IT deployment, non-standard protocol for legacy customer integration), the requesting BU submits a structured exception request. The agent evaluates risk impact and proposes mitigations. BU Architecture Council can approve BU-scoped exceptions; cross-BU exceptions require ARB approval. All exceptions are time-bounded with mandatory review dates.
  • Architecture Change Management: Any modification to baseline or target architecture follows a scoped change process. BU-local changes (affecting only one BU's product architecture) are approved by the BU Architecture Council. Cross-BU changes (affecting shared platforms like Lumada, SAP, or establishing group-wide standards) require ARB approval. The Impact Analysis Agent identifies all affected elements via graph traversal across BU boundaries, producing a change impact report.
  • Agent Registration & Certification: New agents must be registered in the MCP Tool Catalog with defined scope (which BUs and repository layers they can access), trust level, and permitted actions. Certification requires passing a validation suite including: correct handling of IEC 62443 safety boundaries, proper escalation on grid-critical elements, accurate audit logging, and correct multi-regime compliance assessment. The AI Ethics Board approves trust level promotions from advisory to semi-autonomous.

sections.heading: RACI Matrix
sections.content:
Responsibilities for key governance activities, reflecting the federated structure where BU Domain Architects have authority over BU-scoped decisions while the ARB governs cross-BU concerns.

ActivityChief ArchitectBU Domain ArchitectsAI/Agent ArchitectARBBU Architecture CouncilAI Ethics BoardCTO
Approve BU-local architecture decisionIRCIAII
Approve cross-BU architecture decisionRCCAIII
Approve shared platform change (Lumada, SAP)RCCACII
Register new agentCIRIIAI
Promote agent trust levelCIRCIAI
Approve OT architecture changeRCCACCI
Grant BU-scoped exceptionIRCIAII
Grant cross-BU exceptionRCCAIII
Review compliance report (per regime)ARRIIII

R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed

sections.heading: Agent Governance
sections.content:
Agent governance extends traditional architecture governance with four specific mechanisms, designed for a federated environment where agents may operate across BU boundaries and must respect different regulatory regimes per region:

  • Agent Registration: Every agent operating within the EA function is registered in the MCP Tool Catalog with a unique identifier, defined scope (which BUs, repository layers, and element types it can access), permitted actions (read/query/propose/execute), assigned trust level, and regional access permissions (an agent approved for European operations may not automatically operate on North American assets subject to NERC CIP).
  • Guardrail Definition: Each agent has explicit guardrails encoded as machine-readable rules in the graph repository. Guardrails define: hard boundaries (actions always blocked — e.g., modifying MicroSCADA protection settings, altering HVDC control parameters, changing IEC 62443 zone assignments), soft boundaries (actions requiring human approval — e.g., proposing cross-BU architecture changes, recommending platform convergence actions), and open scope (actions the agent can perform autonomously — e.g., running analysis queries, generating compliance reports, monitoring repository health).
  • Audit Trail: Every agent action is logged to an immutable audit ledger in the graph repository. Each log entry captures: timestamp, agent ID, action type, affected BU(s) and region(s), affected elements, input context, output produced, confidence score, applicable regulatory regime, and whether a human reviewed the output. Audit data is retained per regional data retention requirements.
  • Human Oversight Escalation: Agents escalate to human architects when: confidence score falls below threshold (configurable per BU and action type), action touches safety-critical elements (any element within IEC 62443 OT zones), action would affect more than 50 elements across BU boundaries (high blast radius), action involves a regulatory regime the agent is not certified for, or agent detects conflicting principles that require human judgement (e.g., platform convergence vs. BU autonomy).

sections.heading: Success Factors & KPIs
sections.content:
Architecture governance effectiveness is measured against six key success factors, tracked quarterly and reported to the CTO.

Success FactorMetricTargetMeasurement
Governance ParticipationARB and BU Council attendance rate>85% across all bodiesMeeting attendance records; regional participation tracked separately
Decision ThroughputAverage time from architecture proposal to decision<10 business days (BU-scoped); <20 (cross-BU)Decision log timestamps in graph repository
Compliance Coverage% of architecture changes with automated compliance assessment100% within 12 monthsCompliance Agent activity logs per regulatory regime
Exception ManagementActive exceptions; % reviewed on scheduleAll time-bounded; 100% reviewed before expiryException register in graph repository
Agent Trust ProgressionAgents promoted from advisory to semi-autonomousAt least 2 agents at semi-autonomous by month 12Agent registration records and AI Ethics Board minutes
Stakeholder SatisfactionQuarterly survey of BU stakeholders on governance valueNet positive across all 5 BUsAnonymous survey after each quarterly executive review

    properties:
      trigger: Solution building block enters design phase (any BU)
      agent_role: Compliance Agent runs regime-specific rule checks
      human_role: Architects review compliance report per BU/region
      output: Compliance assessment tagged by BU and regulatory regime

    type: BusinessProcess
    name: Exception Process
    properties:

      scope_rules:
        bu_scoped: BU Architecture Council approves
        cross_bu: ARB approves
      agent_role: Agent evaluates risk impact and proposes mitigations
      output: Time-bounded exception with mandatory review date

  - id: proc-change-mgmt
    type: BusinessProcess
    name: Architecture Change Management
    properties:
      trigger: Modification to baseline or target architecture
      scope_rules:
        bu_local: BU Architecture Council approves
        cross_bu: ARB approves
        shared_platform: ARB approves (Lumada, SAP, group standards)
      agent_role: Impact Analysis Agent traces cross-BU affected elements
      output: Scoped change impact report and approval record

  - id: proc-agent-registration
    type: BusinessProcess
    name: Agent Registration & Certification
    stereotype: agentic-process
    properties:
      trigger: New agent deployment request
      steps:
        - Register in MCP Tool Catalog with BU/region scope
        - Define permitted actions and regulatory regime access
        - Run validation suite (IEC 62443 boundaries, escalation, audit)
        - AI Ethics Board approves trust level
      output: Certified agent entry with BU and regional permissions

  # Agent Governance Mechanisms
  - id: mech-guardrails
    type: Constraint
    name: Agent Guardrail Framework
    stereotype: agentic-governance
    properties:
      hard_boundaries: Modify MicroSCADA protection, alter HVDC params, change IEC 62443 zones
      soft_boundaries: Cross-BU architecture changes, platform convergence proposals
      open_scope: Analysis queries, compliance reports, repository health monitoring

  - id: mech-audit-trail
    type: Artifact
    name: Agent Audit Ledger
    stereotype: agentic-governance
    properties:
      storage: Immutable graph repository
      fields:
        - timestamp
        - agent_id
        - action_type
        - affected_bus
        - affected_regions
        - affected_elements
        - confidence_score
        - regulatory_regime
        - human_reviewed

  - id: mech-escalation
    type: BusinessProcess
    name: Human Oversight Escalation
    stereotype: agentic-governance
    properties:
      triggers:
        - Confidence below threshold (configurable per BU)
        - IEC 62443 OT zone elements affected
        - Blast radius exceeds 50 elements across BU boundaries
        - Uncertified regulatory regime
        - Conflicting principles (e.g., convergence vs BU autonomy)

worked_deliverables.name: Request for Architecture Work
worked_deliverables.summary: Formal request initiating the AI Transformation Programme — a CHF 3.2M, 18-month company-wide initiative sponsored by the CTO to assess AI readiness across all five BUs and establish the architecture blueprint for an agentic operating model.

sections.heading: Trigger
sections.content: The AI Transformation Programme is triggered by the CTO's strategic question: how does the company need to adjust to leverage AI? Three converging forces make this urgent. First, competitors are embedding AI into grid management and asset monitoring products — the organisation risks losing technology leadership if it does not accelerate AI adoption across all five BUs. Second, the EU AI Act (effective 2025–2026) requires classification and governance of all AI systems deployed in critical infrastructure, affecting Nostradamus, Lumada APM analytics, and any future agent-based capabilities. Third, the organisation's heterogeneous IT landscape — different platforms, different maturity levels, different tooling across BUs and regions — prevents effective AI deployment at scale. Incremental, BU-by-BU improvement is insufficient; a company-wide architecture transformation is required to create the unified foundation that AI adoption demands.

sections.heading: Scope & Objectives
sections.content:
• Objective 1: Conduct an AI readiness assessment across all five BUs — evaluating data readiness, platform maturity, process automation potential, and regulatory compliance posture for AI deployment (Grid Automation, Grid Integration, HV Products, Transformers, Service)
  • Objective 2: Establish a harmonised reference architecture spanning the product and digital platform landscape — defining how Lumada APM, MicroSCADA X, Network Manager EMS, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, and SAP interoperate, with standard integration patterns that AI agents can leverage
  • Objective 3: Build an AI use case portfolio with at least 3 production-ready use cases per BU (15 total) — from Nostradamus-based demand forecasting in Grid Integration to TXpert predictive maintenance in Transformers to agent-assisted architecture governance in the EA function
  • Objective 4: Implement a graph-native EA repository (Neo4j), migrating existing Sparx EA content (~40% coverage) and extending it to cover all five BUs and four operating regions with full ArchiMate 3.2 metamodel
  • Objective 5: Deploy agent-enabled architecture governance — agents that continuously validate compliance across multiple regulatory regimes (NIS2, NERC CIP, EU AI Act), perform cross-BU impact analysis, and support federated architecture decision-making via 32 MCP tools
  • Objective 6: Reach Level 4 architecture maturity (Quantitatively Managed) across all five dimensions within 18 months, building on the current Level 2–3 baseline

sections.heading: Sponsorship
sections.content:
Executive Sponsor: CTO — accountable for programme outcomes, budget authority, executive steering, and alignment with the Group Executive Team's technology strategy.

Programme Director: Chief Enterprise Architect (Marcus Lindberg) — accountable for delivery across all five BUs, repository migration, agent framework deployment, and architecture maturity progression.

BU Stakeholders: VP of each BU (Grid Automation, Grid Integration, HV Products, Transformers, Service) — accountable for BU-specific AI readiness, use case identification, and benefit realisation within their P&L scope.

Programme Budget: CHF 3.2M over 18 months, allocated as: Personnel (50% — CHF 1.6M for 2 new domain architects, AI/Agent Architect, 2 EA analysts, plus temporary data engineering support), Tooling (25% — CHF 800K for Neo4j Enterprise, MCP server infrastructure, graph migration tools, Lumada integration development), Consulting (15% — CHF 480K for specialist ArchiMate advisory, IEC 62443 security assessment, and EU AI Act compliance guidance), Regional Travel & Coordination (5% — CHF 160K for cross-region workshops in NA and APAC), Contingency (5% — CHF 160K).

Timeline: 18 months from initiation with quarterly milestone reviews. Phase gates aligned to ADM iteration cycles and BU planning calendars.

sections.heading: Expected Outcomes
sections.content:
• AI Use Case Portfolio: 15 production AI use cases across all BUs — Nostradamus forecasting (Grid Integration), Lumada APM predictive maintenance (Service, Transformers), MicroSCADA anomaly detection (Grid Automation), automated quality inspection (HV Products), architecture advisory agent (EA function) — with EU AI Act risk classification for each
  • Harmonised Reference Architecture: Unified view of how Lumada, MicroSCADA, Network Manager, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, and SAP interoperate across BUs and regions, with standard integration patterns and API specifications documented in the graph repository
  • Graph-Native EA Repository: All architecture knowledge migrated from Sparx EA to Neo4j with full BU and regional coverage — impact analysis time reduced from days to seconds; cross-BU dependency analysis enabled by graph traversal; 32 MCP tools for agent consumption
  • Agent-Enabled Governance: Continuous multi-regime compliance monitoring replacing quarterly manual reviews; federated governance model with automated enforcement of architecture principles across all five BUs and four regions
  • Architecture Maturity Level 4: Quantitative architecture management with measurable KPIs — from Level 2–3 baseline to Level 4 across process, people, content, governance, and tools dimensions

sections.heading: Constraints & Risks
sections.content:
RiskProbabilityImpactMitigation
BU resistance to central architecture governance — BUs with P&L autonomy may resist group-wide standards they perceive as constraining product development speedHighHighFederated governance model: BU Architecture Councils retain authority for BU-scoped decisions; ARB governs only cross-BU and platform decisions; demonstrate value through early cross-BU integration wins
Talent gaps — 2 domain architect positions (HV Products, Transformers) and AI/Agent Architect role must be filled; Neo4j and MCP expertise scarceHighHighRecruitment starts immediately in parallel with programme setup; interim coverage from consulting partners; structured knowledge transfer plan; internal training programme for graph-native EA methods
Legacy platform integration complexity — MicroSCADA X, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, and various regional IT systems have different integration capabilities and protocolsHighMediumPhased integration starting with BUs that have highest maturity (Grid Automation, Grid Integration, Service); standard API layer via Lumada as integration hub; BU-specific adaptation where needed
Regional regulatory divergence — EU AI Act, NIS2, NERC CIP, India CERT-In, and China cybersecurity law require different compliance approaches that may conflictMediumHighMulti-regime compliance framework in repository; per-region compliance rule sets; legal review of cross-region conflicts; regional CISO coordination
Data quality and availability across BUs — some BUs (HV Products, Transformers) have limited digital data; sensor coverage varies across manufacturing sites and installed baseMediumHighData Readiness Assessment as early Phase B/C deliverable per BU; pragmatic starting points for BUs with lower data maturity; data quality improvement roadmap per BU
Sparx EA migration risk — existing models may have inconsistencies, orphan elements, or non-standard extensions that complicate graph migrationMediumMediumMigration agent validates and cleanses during import; parallel running of Sparx EA and Neo4j for 6 months; manual review of flagged inconsistencies
Agent trust and adoption — architects and BU stakeholders may resist AI agent participation in architecture governanceMediumMediumGraduated trust model: advisory-only for first 6 months; demonstrate value through concrete examples; BU-level champions programme; transparent audit trails
Scope pressure from adjacent initiatives — North America expansion programme, offshore wind projects, and customer digital services initiatives will want architecture supportMediumMediumStrict scope governance through ARB; adjacent initiatives queued for next ADM cycle; core platform and repository work benefits all future initiatives

sections.heading: Approval
sections.content: This Request for Architecture Work has been reviewed and endorsed by the Architecture Review Board in March 2025. CTO sign-off confirms executive sponsorship, CHF 3.2M budget allocation, and authority to proceed with the Preliminary Phase and first ADM cycle. The programme commences with the assembly of the expanded EA team (recruitment of 2 domain architects and AI/Agent Architect), repository migration planning, and initial BU engagement workshops. A formal Architecture Vision (Phase A) cross-BU workshop will be held in Zurich within the first 6 weeks, with virtual participation from North America and Asia Pacific regional representatives.

sections.heading: Additional Information
sections.content:
Related Documents:

  • Organisational Model for Enterprise Architecture — EA team structure and BU scope
  • Architecture Governance Framework — governance bodies, processes, and RACI
  • Tailored Architecture Framework — federated ADM execution approach
  • Principles Catalog — 10 architecture principles governing all decisions
  • Business Principles, Goals, and Drivers — business context driving this work

Governance Checkpoints:

Target State ReviewMonth 18CTO + Group Executive TeamLevel 4 maturity; 15 AI use cases in production; continuous compliance operational
