worked_deliverables.name: Architecture of the EA Capability
worked_deliverables.summary: The meta-architecture of the EA function itself — defining how existing processes are augmented with AI, how the partial repository is migrated to graph-native, and how four specialised agents support architecture governance across all five BUs.

sections.heading: Meta-Architecture
sections.content:
graph TD
    classDef layer fill:#f1f5f9,stroke:#94a3b8,color:#1e293b
    GOV["Governance Layer — ARB, AI Ethics Board, 5 BU Councils"]:::layer
    PROC["Process Layer — Federated ADM, Central + BU-Parallel"]:::layer
    AGENT["Agent Framework — Advisory, Compliance, Repository, Impact"]:::layer
    REPO["Repository Layer — Neo4j Graph, 32 MCP Tools"]:::layer
    GOV -->|controls| AGENT
    GOV -->|gates| PROC
    PROC -->|reads/writes| REPO
    AGENT -->|accesses via MCP| REPO
    AGENT -->|executes| PROC

The EA Capability is itself an architecture — a system of interacting components that must be designed, governed, and evolved. For this organisation, the EA Capability builds on what exists (European ARB, three domain architects, partial Sparx EA repository, informal BU architecture councils) and extends it with AI augmentation. The meta-architecture comprises four layers: Process Layer (existing ADM execution, now federated across BUs with agent participation), Repository Layer (Sparx EA content migrated to Neo4j graph, extended to all five BUs and four regions), Agent Framework (four specialised agents accessing the repository via MCP), and Governance Layer (existing European ARB globalised, new AI Ethics Board, formalised BU Architecture Councils). These four layers interact continuously: agents execute process steps, read and write to the repository, and are themselves subject to governance controls. The meta-architecture is self-describing — it is stored in the same graph repository it governs, enabling agents to reason about the EA function itself and track its maturity progression from Level 2–3 to Level 4.

sections.heading: EA Process Layer
sections.content:
The ADM phases are executed as a blend of human-led and agent-assisted activities, adapted for the federated BU structure. Governance phases run centrally; domain phases run in parallel across the five BUs. Each phase has defined entry criteria, activities, deliverables, and exit criteria — all stored in the graph repository as executable process definitions.

  • Phase Execution: Central phases (Preliminary, A, E/F, G, H) are led by the Chief Enterprise Architect with cross-BU workshops. Domain phases (B, C, D) run as 2-week agile sprints per BU, with agents generating initial drafts (capability maps per BU, data models for Lumada/SAP/MicroSCADA, technology topologies per region) that BU domain architects review and refine
  • Agent Participation Model: Each activity within a phase is classified as agent-autonomous (data collection from Lumada/SAP APIs, cross-BU pattern detection, repository completeness checking), agent-assisted (cross-BU gap analysis, multi-regime compliance review, impact assessment across BU boundaries), or human-led (BU stakeholder workshops, strategic platform decisions, governance exception approvals)
  • Iteration Cadence: Full ADM cycles run quarterly aligned to BU planning cycles; micro-iterations within phases run bi-weekly; continuous activities (multi-regime compliance monitoring, cross-BU change detection) run in real-time
  • Quality Gates: Each phase completion requires: all mandatory deliverables produced (per BU for domain phases, cross-BU integration view for central phases), compliance checks passed per applicable regulatory regime (agent-verified), BU and central stakeholder sign-off captured, and repository updated with all new elements and relationships tagged by BU and region

sections.heading: Repository Layer
sections.content:
The graph repository evolves from the existing partial Sparx EA catalog into the single source of truth for all architecture knowledge across five BUs and four regions. It is not a passive store — it is an active knowledge graph that agents query, traverse, and update continuously.

  • Database: Neo4j 5.x Enterprise hosted in Zurich, migrated from Sparx EA (~380 existing elements) and extended with BU-specific and regional content. Full ArchiMate 3.2 metamodel — 60 element types, approximately 300 relationship rules enforced as database constraints, 7 architectural layers, and 8 AI-specific stereotypes
  • MCP Server: 32 tools exposed via Model Context Protocol — 8 query tools (element search with BU/region filtering, cross-BU path traversal, pattern matching, viewpoint rendering, layer filtering, relationship listing, full-text search, platform catalog lookup), 8 mutation tools (create element with BU tagging, update properties, create relationship, delete element, merge duplicates across BUs, bulk import from Lumada/SAP, clone subgraph, version snapshot), 8 analysis tools (cross-BU impact analysis, gap detection per BU, dependency mapping, complexity scoring, multi-regime compliance check, BU coverage analysis, health scoring, what-if simulation), 8 validation tools (schema conformance, relationship rule validation, BU completeness scoring, principle evaluation, naming convention check, lifecycle state validation, orphan detection, circular dependency detection)
  • Content: ~1,000 elements at initialisation — 380 migrated from Sparx EA, 120 from platform catalog (Lumada, MicroSCADA, Network Manager, TXpert, MACH/IdentiQ, SAP), 500+ from reference models (IEC 61850, CIM, IEC 62443, regulatory catalog). Growing as BU-parallel ADM phases produce new content. Every element carries lifecycle metadata, BU ownership, regional applicability, provenance (migrated-from-sparx, created-by-human, or proposed-by-agent with confidence score)

sections.heading: Agent Framework
sections.content:
Four specialised agents operate within the EA Capability, each with defined scope, trust level, and permitted actions. Agents are designed for the federated environment — they can operate within a single BU or across BU boundaries depending on the task, with appropriate governance escalation.

Agent RoleScopeTrust LevelKey Capabilities
Architecture Advisory AgentBusiness and Strategy layers — BU capability models, cross-BU shared capabilities, process architecture, stakeholder analysisSemi-autonomousGenerates BU capability maps from requirements, performs cross-BU gap analysis between baseline and target, identifies platform convergence opportunities (e.g., duplicate asset management tools across Grid Automation and Service), drafts stakeholder impact assessments for cross-BU initiatives
Compliance AgentAll layers — multi-regime compliance and standards verification across BUs and regionsSupervised-autonomousEvaluates every architecture change against principles catalog and applicable regulatory regime (NIS2 for European deployments, NERC CIP for North American, EU AI Act for all AI use cases), generates regime-specific compliance reports, flags non-conformance with BU and regional context
Repository AgentRepository metadata — schema integrity, data quality, BU coverage completenessAutonomousMonitors repository health per BU (orphan elements, missing relationships, stale metadata, migration gaps from Sparx EA), enforces naming conventions across BUs, manages versioning, performs automated cleanup, tracks BU coverage metrics against target
Impact Analysis AgentAll layers — cross-BU dependency and change impact assessmentSemi-autonomousTraces change propagation through graph relationships across BU boundaries (e.g., a Lumada platform change affecting Grid Automation, Service, and Transformers), calculates blast radius for proposed changes, identifies affected BU stakeholders, generates change impact reports with risk scores and regulatory implications per region

sections.heading: Change & Adoption
sections.content:
Transitioning the EA capability from Level 2–3 to Level 4 requires deliberate change management across five business units with different starting maturity levels and organisational cultures.

  • BU Champions Programme: One architecture champion per BU (ideally the Domain Architect) leads local adoption. Champions receive early access to the graph repository and agent tools, participate in pilot use cases, and advocate within BU leadership teams
  • Training & Enablement: Three training tracks — (1) Graph-native EA methods for all architects (Neo4j Cypher, ArchiMate viewpoints, repository workflow), (2) Agent collaboration for domain architects (MCP tools, agent output review, trust level management), (3) Executive briefings for BU VPs and regional IT leads (value proposition, governance participation, compliance benefits)
  • Communication Cadence: Monthly EA newsletter highlighting cross-BU wins, agent-assisted analysis examples, and maturity progress per BU. Quarterly stakeholder roadshows with live demonstrations. Executive dashboard with real-time repository and agent activity metrics
  • Resistance Mitigation: BUs with lower starting maturity (HV Products, Transformers at Level 2) receive additional hands-on support — dedicated onboarding sessions, co-development of initial BU capability maps, and lighter initial governance burden (reduced reporting for first 6 months)
  • Success Stories: Early wins documented and shared across BUs — e.g., Compliance Agent identifying a cross-BU NIS2 gap that manual review missed, or Impact Analysis Agent tracing a Lumada platform change across three BUs in seconds rather than days

sections.heading: Maturity Roadmap
sections.content:
The EA Capability matures through three stages over 18 months, building on the existing Level 2–3 baseline rather than starting from scratch.

MilestoneLevelTimelineWhat Changes
Current StateLevel 2–3 — MixedTodayEuropean ARB operational. 3 of 5 BU domain architects in place. ~40% of application landscape catalogued in Sparx EA (Europe only). Architecture processes executed inconsistently across BUs — Grid Automation and Service at Level 3, HV Products and Transformers at Level 2. No agent participation. Manual governance via quarterly reviews in Europe; no formal governance in other regions. Architecture decisions not systematically traceable.
Target State 1Level 3 — Defined9 monthsAll 5 BU domain architects in place. Graph repository operational with Sparx EA content migrated and BU platform catalogs loaded. ARB globalised with virtual participation from all regions. BU Architecture Councils formalised in all 5 BUs. Agent framework deployed with Architecture Advisory and Compliance agents in advisory mode. Continuous compliance monitoring active for NIS2 and NERC CIP. YAML twins generated for all new deliverables. AI Use Case Register populated for all 5 BUs.
Target State 2Level 4 — Quantitatively Managed18 monthsArchitecture outcomes measured with quantitative KPIs per BU and region. Agents operating at semi-autonomous trust level, generating and proposing cross-BU architecture changes. Multi-regime compliance fully automated (NIS2, NERC CIP, EU AI Act). 15 AI use cases in production across all 5 BUs. Full BU and regional coverage in graph repository (target: 90%+ of application landscape). Predictive analytics on architecture health, technical debt, and platform convergence progress. Agent-to-agent collaboration on cross-BU analysis tasks.

phases.togaf_summary: The Preliminary Phase describes the preparation and initiation activities required to create an Architecture Capability including customisation of the TOGAF framework and definition of Architecture Principles.
objectives: Determine the Architecture Capability desired by the organisation: review the organisational context, identify and scope affected enterprise organisations, identify established frameworks and processes that intersect, establish Capability Maturity target

Establish the Architecture Capability: define and establish the Organisational Model for Enterprise Architecture, define the detailed process and resources for Architecture Governance, select and implement supporting tools, define the Architecture Principles
steps: Define and establish Enterprise Architecture team and organisation
Tailor the TOGAF framework and, if any, other selected architecture frameworks
Develop a strategy and implementation plan for tools and techniques

phases.agentic_shift: Principles become machine-enforceable rules that agents evaluate against every architecture decision. The organisational model extends to define agent roles alongside human roles. The YAML twin makes each principle queryable, traceable, and automatically enforceable.

deliverable.name: Principles Catalog
deliverable.description: The Principles Catalog lists architecture principles that guide all design decisions. The traditional version is a document table with name, statement, rationale, and implications. The YAML twin adds ArchiMate Principle element IDs and machine-readable enforcement rules so agents can validate every architecture decision against the defined principles.
deliverable.what_it_produces: A principles-catalog.yaml with ArchiMate Principle elements, each carrying an enforcement rule that agents can evaluate automatically.

fields.label: Organisation
fields.default: Energy Technology Corp

fields.label: Industry
fields.default: Energy

fields.label: Architecture Principles
fields.default:
Cloud First | All new workloads default to cloud-native deployment | Reduce infrastructure costs and enable global scalability | New projects must evaluate cloud-native options before on-premises
API First | All capabilities exposed through well-defined APIs | Enable integration, support agent access, reduce point-to-point coupling | Every service must publish an API specification in the catalog
Agent Safety | Safety-critical operations require human approval before execution | Regulatory compliance for critical energy infrastructure | Agent outputs affecting grid operations need human sign-off
Data Sovereignty | All data processing complies with regional regulations | EU data protection requirements and customer trust | Data location must be verified for every new service deployment
Graph-Native Repository | Architecture knowledge stored in graph database | Enable traversal, impact analysis, and agent-queryable architecture | EA repository uses Neo4j with typed ArchiMate relationships
fields.help: Standard TOGAF principle structure (Name, Statement, Rationale, Implications). The YAML twin adds enforcement rules automatically.

v2_maturity.intro: Score your organisation's current EA maturity and set targets across the six Operating Model domains. Expand each domain to assess individual capabilities.

domains.description: Core architecture modelling capabilities across all domains — vision, roadmap, business, application, data, and technology architecture

domains.label: Architecture Repository

domains.label: Architecture Management
domains.description: How the EA function is organised, developed, and measured — team structure, processes, knowledge, and stakeholders

domains.label: Governance, Communication & Change
domains.description: How architecture decisions are made and enforced, how standards are communicated, and how change is managed

domains.label: Risk, Security & Compliance

domains.label: Net-New for the Agentic Organisation

phases.v2_description: The Preliminary Phase establishes the Architecture Capability — the people, processes, tools, governance, and principles needed to conduct architecture work across the enterprise.

v2_objectives.text: Determine the Architecture Capability desired by the organization
sub_items: Review the organizational context for conducting Enterprise Architecture
Identify and scope the elements of the enterprise organizations affected by the Architecture Capability
Identify the established frameworks, methods, and processes that intersect with the Architecture Capability
Define and establish the Organizational Model for Enterprise Architecture
Define and establish the detailed process and resources for Architecture Governance
Select and implement tools that support the Architecture Capability

non_architectural_inputs: Board strategies and business plans, business strategy, IT strategy, business principles, business goals, and business drivers
Major frameworks operating in the business (e.g., project/portfolio management)
Governance and legal frameworks, including Architecture Governance strategy
architectural_inputs: Organizational Model for Enterprise Architecture — scope of organizations impacted, maturity assessment, gaps and resolution approach, roles and responsibilities, budget requirements, governance and support strategy
Existing Architecture Framework, if any — architecture method, architecture content, configured and deployed tools, Architecture Principles, Architecture Repository

v2_steps.name: Scope the Enterprise Organizations Impacted
v2_steps.description: Identify the enterprise units affected by the Architecture Capability.
sub_steps: Identify core enterprise units — those most affected and achieving most value
Identify soft enterprise units — those who will see change but are not directly affected
Identify extended enterprise units — those outside the scoped enterprise who will be affected
Identify communities involved — stakeholders in groups or communities
Identify governance involved, including legal frameworks and geographies

v2_steps.name: Confirm Governance and Support Frameworks
v2_steps.description: Assess existing governance and support models to understand their shape and content, and determine how architectural material is brought under governance.

v2_steps.name: Define and Establish EA Team and Organization
v2_steps.description: Set up the EA capability including roles, responsibilities, and organizational structure.
sub_steps: Determine existing enterprise and business capability
Conduct an EA/business change maturity assessment, if required
Identify gaps in existing work areas
Allocate key roles and responsibilities for EA Capability management and governance
Define requests for change to existing business programs and projects
Review and agree with sponsors and board

v2_steps.name: Identify and Establish Architecture Principles
v2_steps.description: Define a set of Architecture Principles appropriate to the enterprise, based on business principles — critical in setting the foundation for Architecture Governance.

v2_steps.name: Tailor the Architecture Framework
v2_steps.description: Determine what tailoring is required for terminology, process, and content.
sub_steps: Terminology Tailoring — produce an agreed terminology set for architectural content
Process Tailoring — align ADM processes to external frameworks and organization-specific tasks
Content Tailoring — tailor content structure for organization-specific requirements

v2_steps.name: Develop Strategy for Tools and Techniques
v2_steps.description: Create a tools strategy reflecting the understanding and level of formality required by stakeholders.

phases.name: Architecture Vision
phases.togaf_summary: Phase A: Architecture Vision describes the initial phase of an architecture development cycle. It includes information about defining the scope of the architecture development initiative, identifying the stakeholders, creating the Architecture Vision, and obtaining approval to proceed with the architecture development.
objectives: Develop a high-level aspirational vision of the capabilities and business value to be delivered as a result of the proposed Enterprise Architecture
Obtain approval for a Statement of Architecture Work that defines a programme of works to develop and deploy the architecture outlined in the Architecture Vision
steps: Identify stakeholders, concerns, and business requirements
Confirm and elaborate business goals, business drivers, and constraints
Confirm and elaborate Architecture Principles, including business principles
Define the Target Architecture value propositions and KPIs
Identify the business transformation risks and mitigation activities
Develop Statement of Architecture Work; secure approval

togaf_deliverables.desc: Architecture project description and scope, overview of Architecture Vision, project plan and schedule

togaf_deliverables.name: Architecture Vision

phases.agentic_shift: The stakeholder map extends to include agent interactions — which agents serve which stakeholders and how. The YAML twin captures power/interest as structured data enabling automated stakeholder engagement tracking. Agents can identify which stakeholders must be consulted for any given architecture decision.

deliverable.name: Stakeholder Map Matrix
deliverable.description: The Stakeholder Map captures who is affected by the architecture, what they care about, and how they should be engaged. The traditional version is a power/interest grid. The YAML twin adds ArchiMate BusinessActor elements with structured concern mappings and agent interaction definitions — which agents communicate with which stakeholders.
deliverable.what_it_produces: A stakeholder-map.yaml matrix with typed stakeholder elements, power/interest classification, and agent interaction mappings.

objectives: Develop the Target Business Architecture that describes how the enterprise needs to operate to achieve the business goals, and respond to the strategic drivers set out in the Architecture Vision, in a way that addresses the Statement of Architecture Work and stakeholder concerns
Identify candidate Architecture Roadmap components based upon gaps between the Baseline and Target Business Architectures
steps: Select reference models, viewpoints, and tools
Resolve impacts across the Architecture Landscape

phases.agentic_shift: Process catalogs gain an agent dimension — every step is assessed for agent suitability (autonomous, assisted, human-only). The YAML twin uses ArchiMate BusinessProcess elements connected by TRIGGERING relationships, with agent readiness scores and decision type classifications layered on top.

deliverable.name: Process Flow Catalog
deliverable.description: The Process Flow Catalog captures business processes as a sequence of steps with performers, inputs, and outputs. The traditional version is a table or swim-lane diagram. The YAML twin structures each step as an ArchiMate BusinessProcess element with typed relationships and adds agent suitability scores — which steps can agents perform, assist with, or must humans handle.
deliverable.what_it_produces: A process-catalog.yaml with ArchiMate BusinessProcess elements connected by TRIGGERING relationships, annotated with agent readiness scores.

fields.label: Gap Analysis
fields.format: Capability | Baseline State | Target State | Gap Description | Priority (high/medium/low)
fields.default:
Predictive Maintenance | Reactive maintenance only | AI-driven prediction 30+ days ahead | No ML models, sensors not integrated into analytics | high
Real-time Asset Monitoring | Periodic manual inspection | Continuous IoT monitoring with alerts | Sensors deployed but not connected to analytics platform | high

fields.help: Baseline vs. target per capability. YAML twin adds agent readiness scores (1–5) and estimates agent augmentation value.

phases.name: Migration Planning
phases.togaf_summary: Phase F: Migration Planning addresses how to move from the Baseline to the Target Architectures by finalising a detailed Implementation and Migration Plan.
objectives: Finalise the Architecture Roadmap and the supporting Implementation and Migration Plan
Ensure that the Implementation and Migration Plan is co-ordinated with the enterprise's approach to managing and implementing change in the enterprise's overall change portfolio
Ensure that the business value and cost of work packages and Transition Architectures is understood by key stakeholders
steps: Confirm management framework interactions for Implementation and Migration Plan
Assign a business value to each work package
Estimate resource requirements, project timings, and availability/delivery vehicle
Prioritise the migration projects through the conduct of a cost/benefit assessment and risk validation
Confirm Architecture Roadmap and update Architecture Definition Document
Complete the Implementation and Migration Plan
Complete the architecture development cycle and document lessons learned

The YAML twin structures each compliance check as a typed element with machine-readable verification rules — agents can monitor compliance continuously and flag violations in real-time rather than waiting for quarterly reviews.

The traditional version is a checklist document with pass/fail assessments. The YAML twin structures each compliance item as a typed element with machine-readable monitoring rules — enabling agents to verify compliance continuously and automatically, not just at review checkpoints.

phases.agentic_shift: Change requests gain automated impact analysis via graph traversal — an agent can instantly trace all downstream effects of a proposed change across every architecture layer. The YAML twin structures the change request with element-level impact references, enabling agents to detect architecture drift and generate change requests proactively.

deliverable.name: Architecture Change Request

deliverable.description: The Change Request documents a proposed architecture change with description, rationale, and impact assessment. The traditional version is a form document. The YAML twin structures the change with ArchiMate element references for every impacted component, enabling agents to perform automated impact analysis via graph traversal — tracing effects across all architecture layers.
deliverable.what_it_produces: A change-request.yaml with a ChangeRequest element and ImpactAssessment entries referencing specific architecture elements by ID.

phases.name: Requirements Management
phases.togaf_summary: Requirements Management operates the process of managing architecture requirements throughout the ADM.
objectives: Ensure that the Requirements Management process is sustained and operates for all relevant ADM phases
Manage architecture requirements identified during any execution of the ADM cycle or a phase
Ensure that relevant architecture requirements are available for use by each phase as the phase is executed
steps: Identify requirements and document them in the Architecture Requirements Specification and Requirements Repository
Establish baseline requirements: determine priorities, confirm stakeholder agreement
Identify new and changed requirements (remove, add, modify, re-assess priorities)
Identify changed requirements and record priorities; generate Requirements Impact Statement
Assess impact of changed requirements on current and previous phases; determine whether to implement or defer
Implement requirements arising from Phase H
Implement change in the current phase
Assess and revise gap analysis for past phases

phases.agentic_shift: Requirements become graph nodes linked to the architecture elements they constrain. The YAML twin structures each requirement as an ArchiMate Requirement element with explicit traceability to the elements that satisfy it — enabling agents to automatically verify coverage, detect orphaned requirements, and flag conflicts.

deliverable.name: Requirements Catalog

The traditional version is a spreadsheet with IDs and descriptions. The YAML twin structures each requirement as an ArchiMate Requirement element with explicit REALIZATION links to the elements that satisfy it — enabling automated coverage checking and gap detection by agents.
deliverable.what_it_produces: A requirements-catalog.yaml with Requirement elements, traceability relationships, and automated validation metadata.
